docs.text: Production deploys use a staged rollout with rollback guardrails.

docs.title: Staged Rollout
docs.text:
1. Build image and run CI gates.
  2. Deploy to staging.
  3. Run verification (scripts/verify.ps1) on staging-like workload.
  4. Promote to production only when checks pass.

docs.title: Canary Guidance
docs.text:
• Start at low traffic percentage.
  • Observe:
  • 5xx ratio,
  • p95 latency,
  • fallback rate,
  • model loaded status.
  • Pause or roll back on sustained threshold breach.

docs.title: Rollback Triggers
docs.text:
• Availability or latency SLO burn alerts.
  • Regression in prediction correctness or policy constraints.
  • Incident commander decision for unresolved degradation.

docs.title: Operational Evidence
docs.text:
• Staging verification workflow run logs.
  • Rollback drill log and timestamp.
  • Release ticket with go/no-go approvals.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
Covers restoration of:

  • API service configuration,
  • model artifacts,
  • run manifests and reports,
  • operational configs and dashboards.

docs.title: Targets
docs.text:
• RTO (recovery time objective): 4 hours
  • RPO (recovery point objective): 24 hours

docs.title: Backup Plan
docs.text:
• Daily backup of:
  • outputs/models/
  • outputs/reports/
  • configs/
  • Backup integrity verified daily.

docs.title: Restore Procedure
docs.text:
1. Provision clean staging namespace.
  2. Restore latest backup snapshot.
  3. Run validation:
  4. python scripts/production_readiness_gate.py --mode validate
  5. powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File scripts/verify.ps1 -SkipCorrectness -SkipLoad
  6. Validate API status endpoints and key prediction path.

docs.title: Drill Cadence
docs.text:
• Quarterly DR drill.
  • Record:
  • start/end timestamps,
  • actual RTO/RPO,
  • failures and remediations.

docs.title: Log Levels by Environment
docs.text:
Environment Level Rationale dev DEBUG Full verbosity for local development staging INFO Moderate verbosity for integration prod WARNING Minimal noise; errors + warnings only

Set via HPCOPT_LOG_LEVEL env var or configs/environments/<env>.yaml.

The runtime log level can be changed dynamically via:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/v1/admin/log-level \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "X-API-Key: $API_KEY" \
  -d '{"level": "DEBUG"}'

docs.title: Structured Logging Format
docs.text: All log messages are emitted as structured JSON with: - timestamp (ISO 8601) - level - trace_id (correlation ID) - message - module / function

docs.title: Docker Log Rotation
docs.text:
Configure the Docker JSON file logging driver in docker-compose.yaml or daemon config:

services:
  hpcopt:
    logging:
      driver: json-file
      options:
        max-size: "50m"
        max-file: "5"

Or system-wide in /etc/docker/daemon.json:

{
  "log-driver": "json-file",
  "log-opts": {
    "max-size": "50m",
    "max-file": "5"
  }
}

docs.title: Kubernetes Log Management
docs.text:
In K8s, logs are typically managed by the node-level logging agent (e.g., Fluentd, Fluent Bit, or the default kubelet rotation).

Default kubelet rotation (usually sufficient): - Logs stored in /var/log/pods/ - Rotated when container log file exceeds 10MB (configurable via --container-log-max-size) - Retains 5 rotated files by default (--container-log-max-files)

For centralized logging, deploy a DaemonSet (Fluent Bit → Elasticsearch/Loki):

# See k8s/ directory for ServiceMonitor which feeds Prometheus.
# For log aggregation, deploy Fluent Bit with this output config snippet:
# [OUTPUT]
#     Name  es
#     Match *
#     Host  elasticsearch.logging.svc.cluster.local
#     Port  9200
#     Index hpcopt-logs

docs.title: Volume Management
docs.text:
• Model artifacts: Mount as read-only PVC (hpcopt-models in k8s/deployment.yaml)
  • Temporary files: Use emptyDir ephemeral volumes (cleaned on pod restart)
  • Disk health: The /health endpoint checks free disk space (threshold: HPCOPT_HEALTH_MIN_DISK_FREE_GB)

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Define minimum requirements before an ML scheduling policy recommendation is deployable.

docs.title: Required Inputs
docs.text:
• Baseline simulation report.
  • Candidate simulation report.
  • Fidelity report.
  • Recommendation report.
  • Drift report for current production model.

docs.title: Acceptance Rules
docs.text:
1. Fidelity status must be pass.
  2. Recommendation status must be accepted.
  3. Constraint contract must pass (fairness/starvation limits).
  4. Fallback usage must remain within expected envelope for workload class.
  5. No open high-severity reliability or security issues.

docs.title: Rejection Rules
docs.text:
• Any required report missing.
  • Fidelity fail.
  • Recommendation blocked.
  • Unexplained degradation in primary KPI or fairness.

docs.title: Change Control
docs.text:
• Promotion requires peer review of all reports.
  • Promotion decision recorded in release ticket with report artifact links.
  • Rollback trigger documented before deployment.

docs.title: On-Call and Escalation
docs.text:
• Primary team: platform-team
  • Secondary team: ml-systems-team
  • Escalation target: engineering-manager

docs.title: Control Ownership
docs.text: Control Primary Owner Backup Owner Review Cadence Correctness gates (lint/type/tests) Platform ML Systems Per PR Benchmark regression ML Systems Platform Weekly API load SLO Platform SRE Weekly Fidelity and recommendation gate ML Systems Platform Per release Observability and runbooks SRE Platform Monthly Security controls Security Platform Weekly Deployment safety and rollback SRE Platform Per release Model ops (drift/retrain) ML Systems SRE Weekly Disaster recovery SRE Platform Quarterly drill API compatibility Platform ML Systems Per PR / release

docs.title: Release Approval Roles
docs.text:
• Technical signoff: Platform + ML Systems.
  • Operational signoff: SRE.
  • Security signoff: Security owner.
  • Final go/no-go: Engineering manager.

docs.title: Current Design
docs.text:
The model registry uses JSONL files with advisory file locking for state management:

outputs/registry/
├── runtime_registry.jsonl       # Runtime quantile model entries
├── resource_fit_registry.jsonl  # Resource fit model entries (future)
└── promotion_log.jsonl          # Audit trail of model promotions

docs.title: Write Safety
docs.text:
• Single-writer assumption: Advisory file locks (fcntl.flock on Linux, msvcrt.locking on Windows) prevent concurrent writes from the same host.
  • Append-only: Registry entries are appended, never modified. Promotion/archival is a new entry with updated status.
  • Atomic reads: Not guaranteed. A concurrent read during a write may see a partial line.

docs.title: Risks Under Multi-Instance
docs.text: Risk Severity Mitigation Concurrent writes High Advisory locks don't work across pods Partial line reads Medium Retry + JSON parse validation Split-brain registry High Each pod sees its own copy

docs.title: Recommended Workaround (K8s)
docs.text:
For K8s deployments with multiple API replicas, use a StatefulSet with replicas: 1 for the registry writer process:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: hpcopt-registry-writer
spec:
  replicas: 1           # CRITICAL: single writer
  serviceName: hpcopt-registry
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: registry
          command: ["hpcopt", "serve", "--registry-only"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: registry-data
              mountPath: /app/outputs/registry
  volumeClaimTemplates:
    - metadata:
        name: registry-data
      spec:
        accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
        resources:
          requests:
            storage: 1Gi

docs.title: Migration Path: PostgreSQL Backend
docs.text:
For production deployments requiring horizontal writes and transactional safety:

  1. Schema: Two tables — model_entries (immutable rows) and model_status (current promotion state).
  2. Locking: PostgreSQL row-level locks (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) replace advisory file locks.
  3. Migration: Read existing JSONL entries → insert into PostgreSQL → update config to use SQL backend.
  4. Connection pooling: Use PgBouncer or SQLAlchemy's built-in pool with pool_size=5, max_overflow=10.

docs.title: Alternative: etcd
docs.text: For environments where PostgreSQL is not available: - etcd provides strongly consistent key-value storage - Use etcd leases for model lock management - Suitable for small registry sizes (< 10K entries)

docs.title: Kubernetes Resilience Features
docs.text: HPCOpt ships with three resilience-focused K8s manifests for production hardening:

docs.title: PodDisruptionBudget (k8s/pdb.yaml)
docs.text:
• Ensures at least 1 pod is always available during voluntary disruptions (node drain, cluster upgrades).
  • Protects against accidental eviction of all replicas.
  • Spec: minAvailable: 1.

docs.title: NetworkPolicy (k8s/network-policy.yaml)
docs.text:
• Restricts ingress to only authorized namespaces:
  • ingress-nginx namespace (external traffic via ingress controller),
  • monitoring namespace (Prometheus scraping).
  • Blocks pod-to-pod traffic from other namespaces.
  • Ingress on TCP port 8080.

docs.title: Deployment Lifecycle (k8s/deployment.yaml)
docs.text:
• preStop hook: sleep 5s before SIGTERM to allow in-flight connection draining.
  • Readiness probe: returns 503 when degraded (disk low, shutting down), removing the pod from Service endpoints.
  • Graceful shutdown: drains in-flight requests over the termination grace period.

docs.title: Current Architecture
docs.text:
HPCOpt runs as a single-instance API server by default. Key constraints:

Component Scaling Behavior Rate limiter In-process (per-instance token bucket) Model cache In-process (loaded per instance) Model registry File-based JSONL with advisory locking API keys File-mounted (reads on every request)

docs.title: Multi-Instance Deployment
docs.text: HPCOpt can be deployed with multiple replicas. Each replica: - Loads its own copy of the ML model from the shared PVC - Maintains its own rate-limiting buckets - Reads API keys from the same mounted secret/file

docs.title: What Works
docs.text:
• Read-only prediction endpoints (/v1/runtime/predict, /v1/resource-fit/predict) scale horizontally without coordination.
  • Health/readiness probes are stateless and work per-instance.
  • Prometheus metrics are scraped per-pod via the ServiceMonitor.

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
1. Rate limiting is per-instance: With N replicas, effective rate limit is N × configured limit. For strict global rate limiting, use a Redis-backed limiter (see Future Path below).
  2. Model registry writes are single-writer: The JSONL model registry uses advisory file locks. Only one writer should be active at a time. Use a K8s StatefulSet with replicas: 1 for the registry writer, or migrate to PostgreSQL (see persistent-state.md).
  3. Log-level changes are per-instance: The POST /v1/admin/log-level endpoint only affects the receiving pod.

docs.title: HPA Configuration
docs.text: See k8s/hpa.yaml for the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler configuration: - Min replicas: 2 (for availability) - Max replicas: 8 - Scale-up trigger: CPU > 70% sustained for 60s - Scale-down: Conservative (1 pod per 2 minutes, 5-minute stabilization)

docs.title: Load Testing
docs.text:
Current load tests (tests/load/) run in-process via FastAPI's TestClient, which exercises the handler logic but does not test network I/O, container startup time, or reverse-proxy behavior. For production validation, run an external load test tool (e.g. locust, k6, wrk) against the Docker image:

# Build and run the container
docker build -t hpcopt-api:load .
docker run -d --name hpcopt-load -p 8080:8080 hpcopt-api:load

# Example k6 command
k6 run --vus 50 --duration 60s scripts/load/k6_prediction.js

docs.title: Rate Limit Persistence
docs.text:
The token-bucket rate limiter is in-memory only:

  • State is lost on pod restart.
  • State is not shared across replicas (each pod enforces independently).
  • With N replicas, effective limit = N × configured limit.

For workloads requiring strict global rate limiting, replace the in-memory limiter with a Redis-backed sliding window (see Future Path below).

docs.title: Future Path: Distributed Components
docs.text: Component Current Target Rate limiter In-process token bucket Redis-backed sliding window Model cache Per-instance memory Shared Redis/NFS cache Model registry JSONL + file lock PostgreSQL with row-level lock Session state None (stateless) Redis (if needed)

docs.title: Service Scope
docs.text: This document defines production SLOs for the HPCOpt API service (hpcopt.api.app with supporting modules api.auth, api.rate_limit, api.model_cache, api.deprecation) running on Kubernetes.

docs.title: Availability SLO
docs.text:
• Target: 99.5% monthly availability.
  • Measurement window: rolling 30 days.
  • Availability formula: successful requests / total requests.
  • Successful request: HTTP status <500.

docs.title: Latency SLOs
docs.text:
• GET /health, GET /ready, GET /v1/system/status: p95 < 300ms
  • POST /v1/runtime/predict, POST /v1/resource-fit/predict: p95 < 1.5s

docs.title: Error Budget
docs.text:
• Monthly availability budget: 0.5% downtime/error.
  • Policy when budget burn exceeds threshold:
  • freeze non-critical feature releases,
  • prioritize reliability bug fixes,
  • require rollback readiness for any deploy.

docs.title: Alerting Policy
docs.text:
• Fast burn alert: budget burn rate > 2x over 1h.
  • Slow burn alert: budget burn rate > 1x over 6h.
  • High-severity alert for:
  • sustained 5xx spike,
  • prediction latency p95 breach,
  • fallback spike beyond baseline envelope.

docs.title: Reporting
docs.text:
• Weekly reliability review:
  • SLO attainment,
  • top incidents,
  • corrective actions,
  • open risk items.

docs.title: Alerting Destinations
docs.text:
Production alerts are routed via Prometheus Alertmanager (see k8s/alertmanager-config.yaml):

Severity Destination Repeat Interval critical PagerDuty on-call 1 hour warning Slack #hpcopt-alerts 4 hours info Slack #hpcopt-alerts 24 hours

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
1. Create a PagerDuty service and obtain an integration key.
  2. Create a Slack incoming webhook for #hpcopt-alerts.
  3. Replace the placeholder values in k8s/alertmanager-config.yaml.
  4. Apply: kubectl apply -f k8s/alertmanager-config.yaml

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: HPCOpt includes optional OpenTelemetry instrumentation for distributed tracing of API requests. Tracing is enabled by installing the [tracing] extra and configuring an OTLP collector endpoint.

docs.title: 1. Install the Tracing Extra
docs.text:
pip install hpc-workload-optimizer[tracing]

This adds: - opentelemetry-api - opentelemetry-sdk - opentelemetry-instrumentation-fastapi

docs.title: 2. Configure Environment Variables
docs.text:
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:4317
export OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=hpcopt
export OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER=parentbased_traceidratio
export OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER_ARG=0.1    # sample 10% of traces in prod

docs.title: 3. Deploy an OTLP Collector
docs.text:
See k8s/otel-collector.yaml for a K8s sidecar deployment, or run locally:

docker run -d -p 4317:4317 -p 4318:4318 \
  otel/opentelemetry-collector:latest \
  --config /etc/otel/collector.yaml

docs.title: What Gets Traced
docs.text:
• Every HTTP request (path, method, status, duration)
  • Correlation ID propagation via X-Trace-ID / X-Correlation-ID headers
  • Model loading and prediction latency
  • Rate limiting decisions

docs.title: Sampling Strategy
docs.text: Environment Sampler Rate dev always_on 100% staging parentbased_traceidratio 50% prod parentbased_traceidratio 10%

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text: • p95 latency breaches SLO for core prediction endpoints.

docs.title: Checks
docs.text:
1. Confirm degradation scope by endpoint.
  2. Check CPU/memory saturation and queue depth.
  3. Check model loading state and fallback rate (api/model_cache.py manages the predictor cache).
  4. Check request volume spikes and rate limiting behavior (api/rate_limit.py manages token buckets).
  5. Check for 504 Gateway Timeout responses -- the request timeout (default 30s, configurable via HPCOPT_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_SEC) may be too aggressive for the current load.

docs.title: Mitigation
docs.text:
1. Scale deployment replicas.
  2. Enable temporary request shedding/rate reduction if needed (override via HPCOPT_RATE_LIMIT env var).
  3. Increase request timeout if 504s are legitimate slow predictions (HPCOPT_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_SEC).
  4. Roll back recent deploy if regression introduced.
  5. Force fallback path if model inference is bottlenecked.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• p95 returns below threshold for 30 minutes.
  • 5xx rate returns to baseline.

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text: • 5xx error rate exceeds threshold for 5+ minutes.

docs.title: Checks
docs.text:
1. Identify endpoint(s) returning 5xx. Note: 504 responses indicate request timeout (HPCOPT_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_SEC, default 30s).
  2. Inspect structured logs using X-Trace-ID.
  3. Check upstream dependencies and storage availability.
  4. Check model cache state -- if the predictor failed to load at startup, model_cache.is_loaded() will return False and all predictions use the fallback path.
  5. Correlate with recent config/deploy/model changes.

docs.title: Mitigation
docs.text:
1. Roll back latest deploy if correlated.
  2. Revert risky config changes.
  3. Route to fallback policy if model path failing.
  4. Increase replicas only if resource saturation is causal.

docs.title: Exit Criteria
docs.text:
• 5xx rate below threshold for 30 minutes.
  • Root cause identified and tracked.

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text: Use this runbook when production availability, latency, or correctness is degraded.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Declare incident and assign commander.
  2. Freeze active deploys.
  3. Collect current status:
  4. /health, /ready, /v1/system/status
  5. error rate / latency panels
  6. recent deploy/model promotion history
  7. Classify severity:
  8. Sev1: major outage or severe SLO breach
  9. Sev2: partial degradation
  10. Mitigate:
  11. rollback latest deploy if correlated
  12. switch to safe policy/fallback if model issue
  13. Communicate status every 15 minutes.
  14. Close incident after stability window.
  15. Open postmortem with timeline and action items.

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text: • X-Fallback-Used=true rate exceeds expected baseline envelope.

docs.title: Checks
docs.text:
1. Confirm model directory configured and readable.
  2. Check model staleness and metadata integrity.
  3. Confirm runtime predictor cache state.
  4. Check for drift alerts or model promotion events.

docs.title: Mitigation
docs.text:
1. Restore last known-good model.
  2. If unavailable, keep fallback and throttle risky policy rollout.
  3. Trigger retraining pipeline if drift threshold breached.

docs.title: Exit Criteria
docs.text:
• Fallback rate returns to normal.
  • Model health checks stable for 1 hour.

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text: • New release breaches SLO or causes functional regression.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
• Previous image tag and rollout command available.
  • On-call approver identified.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Pause rollout progression.
  2. Roll back deployment to previous stable image.
  3. Validate:
  4. /health, /ready, /v1/system/status
  5. 5xx and p95 latency recovery
  6. Record incident and rollback timestamp.
  7. Block further promotion until root cause is resolved.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• Service restored within rollback SLO target.
  • No recurring alert after 30-minute observation window.

docs.title: Repository and CI
docs.text:
• Least privilege for repository roles.
  • Branch protection on main.
  • Mandatory PR review before merge.

docs.title: Production Access
docs.text:
• Production access limited to on-call engineers and SRE.
  • All access must be auditable.
  • Emergency elevation requires incident ticket reference.

docs.title: Admin Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
docs.text:
API endpoints under /v1/admin/* require admin-level access controlled via API key prefix:

  • Admin key prefix: API keys must have the admin- prefix (e.g., admin-production-key-12345).
  • Non-admin keys: rejected on admin paths with 403 FORBIDDEN.
  • Current admin endpoints: POST /v1/admin/log-level (dynamic log-level adjustment with audit logging).
  • Audit logging: all admin operations are logged via structured audit trail recording the API key, timestamp, and old→new value.
  • Development mode: when no API keys are configured, all paths are unrestricted.

Implementation: python/hpcopt/api/auth.py (check_admin_auth(), ADMIN_KEY_PREFIX = "admin-").

docs.title: API Input Security
docs.text:
• Request body size limit: 1MB max (returns 413 PAYLOAD_TOO_LARGE).
  • Input bounds: Pydantic models enforce le=, max_length=, and extra="forbid" on all request fields.
  • RFC 7807 error responses: all error responses use Problem Details format with type, title, status, detail, instance fields.

docs.title: Key Practices
docs.text:
• Use short-lived credentials where possible.
  • Remove stale access quarterly.
  • Maintain ownership matrix for privileged operations.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
API keys are loaded by python/hpcopt/utils/secrets.py via load_api_keys(), which checks three sources in priority order:

1. HPCOPT_API_KEYS_FILE env var  -->  reads file (one key per line)
2. /run/secrets/hpcopt_api_keys  -->  Docker/K8s secret mount (auto-detected)
3. HPCOPT_API_KEYS env var       -->  comma-separated (legacy, logs warning)

flowchart LR
  F1["HPCOPT_API_KEYS_FILE"] -->|"priority 1"| LOAD["load_api_keys()"]
  F2["/run/secrets/hpcopt_api_keys"] -->|"priority 2"| LOAD
  F3["HPCOPT_API_KEYS"] -->|"priority 3 (legacy)"| LOAD
  LOAD --> AUTH["api/auth.py\ncheck_api_key_auth()"]
  AUTH --> MW["API middleware\napi/middleware.py"]

docs.title: Key Properties
docs.text:
• Per-request reload: keys are re-read on every API request, enabling rotation without restart.
  • Comment support: lines starting with # are ignored in key files.
  • Graceful degradation: if the configured file is missing, an empty set is returned (auth disabled) with a warning logged.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• No secrets in source control.
  • Use file-based secret injection for API keys (preferred over environment variables).
  • Docker deployments use docker-compose.yaml secrets block with file mount.
  • Kubernetes deployments mount secrets at /run/secrets/hpcopt_api_keys.
  • Rotate production secrets every 90 days or on incident.
  • Legacy HPCOPT_API_KEYS env var is supported for backwards compatibility but triggers a deprecation log.

docs.title: Docker Setup
docs.text:
# docker-compose.yaml
services:
  hpcopt:
    environment:
      HPCOPT_API_KEYS_FILE: /run/secrets/hpcopt_api_keys
    secrets:
      - hpcopt_api_keys

secrets:
  hpcopt_api_keys:
    file: ./secrets/api_keys.txt

Create the key file:

mkdir -p secrets
echo "production-api-key-1" > secrets/api_keys.txt
echo "production-api-key-2" >> secrets/api_keys.txt

docs.title: CI/CD
docs.text:
• Use repository/organization secret store only.
  • Never print secret values in logs.
  • Restrict secret access by workflow scope.
  • Secret scanning runs in CI via gitleaks.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Secret scanning runs on every push/PR in CI.
  • Bandit SAST scans Python code for security issues on every push/PR.
  • Any leaked secret triggers immediate rotation and incident ticket.
  • tests/unit/test_secrets.py validates all three loading paths.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
• Python dependencies
  • Container image
  • Repository secret exposure

docs.title: Controls
docs.text:
1. Dependency audit on CI.
  2. Container scan on release workflow.
  3. Secret scan on CI.

docs.title: Severity Handling
docs.text:
• Critical/High: block release until fixed or formally accepted by security owner.
  • Medium: remediation plan required.
  • Low: backlog with periodic review.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
• Store scan logs/artifacts in release ticket.
  • Record accepted risk exceptions with expiry date.

docs.title: Benchmark Suite
docs.text: Run reproducible comparisons across multiple HPC workload traces and scheduling policies.

docs.title: Running the Full Suite
docs.text:
python scripts/benchmark_suite.py

This will:

  1. Download 3 PWA traces (CTC-SP2, HPC2N, SDSC-SP2)
  2. Convert SWF to JSON format
  3. Run FIFO + EASY_BACKFILL on each via Rust sim-runner
  4. Generate a comparative results table

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
Trace Jobs FIFO p95_BSLD EASY p95_BSLD Improvement CTC-SP2 77,222 195.73 3.94 +98.0% HPC2N 202,870 749.60 60.22 +92.0% SDSC-SP2 54,044 82,865 340.71 +99.6%

Total simulation time

All 6 runs complete in under 1 second on the Rust engine.

Place any SWF file in data/raw/ and update the benchmark script:

TRACES = {
    "my_cluster": {"swf": "data/raw/my_trace.swf", "cpus": 1024},

docs.text: Variable Default Description HPCOPT_API_KEY (required) API authentication key HPCOPT_LOG_LEVEL INFO Logging level HPCOPT_MODEL_DIR outputs/models Path to trained models HPCOPT_FEEDBACK_DIR outputs/feedback Prediction feedback storage

docs.title: Production Checklist
docs.text:
See Production Readiness Checklist for the full list.

Key items:

  • API key configured and rotated

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.11+ — Required for all Python components
  • Rust 1.70+ — Optional, for the Rust simulation engine
  • NVIDIA GPU — Optional, for GPU-accelerated LightGBM training

docs.title: From PyPI
docs.text: pip install hpc-workload-optimizer

docs.title: Optional Extras

pip install hpc-workload-optimizer[lightgbm]

# OpenTelemetry tracing
pip install hpc-workload-optimizer[tracing]

git clone https://github.com/ErenAri/HCP-workload-optimizer.git
cd HCP-workload-optimizer
pip install -e ".[dev,lightgbm]"

The binary will be at rust/target/release/sim-runner (or sim-runner.exe on Windows).

docs.title: Verify Installation

python -c "import hpcopt; print('hpcopt OK')"

# CLI

# Rust  (optional)

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
docker build -t hpcopt .
docker run -p 8080:8080 hpcopt

docs.title: LightGBM Training
docs.text: LightGBM provides 50× faster training compared to sklearn's GradientBoostingRegressor, with identical or better accuracy.

docs.title: Auto-Detection
docs.text:
If LightGBM is installed, it's automatically selected as the default backend:

pip install lightgbm>=4.3.0
# Or with the project extra:
pip install hpc-workload-optimizer[lightgbm]

docs.title: Training
docs.text:
from pathlib import Path
from hpcopt.models.runtime_quantile import train_runtime_quantile_models

result = train_runtime_quantile_models(
    dataset_path=Path("outputs/curated/ctc_sp2_features.parquet"),
    out_dir=Path("outputs/models"),
    model_id="ctc_sp2_lgbm",
    backend="lightgbm",  # or "sklearn" for the original
)

docs.title: GPU Acceleration
docs.text:
If you have an NVIDIA GPU (tested on RTX 2060):

result = train_runtime_quantile_models(
    dataset_path=Path("outputs/curated/ctc_sp2_features.parquet"),
    out_dir=Path("outputs/models"),
    model_id="ctc_sp2_gpu",
    backend="lightgbm",
    hyperparams={"device": "gpu"},
)

When to use GPU

GPU acceleration adds kernel launch overhead. It's faster than CPU for datasets with 500K+ rows. For smaller datasets (under 100K rows), CPU LightGBM is typically faster.

docs.title: Performance Comparison
docs.text: Backend Time p50 MAE Speedup sklearn GBR (CPU) 383.4s 7,889s baseline LightGBM (CPU) 7.6s 7,854s 50× LightGBM (GPU/RTX 2060) 15.5s 7,854s 25×

docs.title: Ensemble Prediction
docs.text:
Combine multiple backends for best accuracy:

from hpcopt.models.ensemble import EnsemblePredictor

ensemble = EnsemblePredictor.from_model_dirs([
    Path("outputs/models/ctc_sp2_sklearn"),
    Path("outputs/models/ctc_sp2_lgbm"),
])

prediction = ensemble.predict_one({"requested_cpus": 4, "user_id": "alice", ...})
# {'p10': 120.5, 'p50': 340.2, 'p90': 890.1}

The ensemble auto-weights models by inverse pinball loss — better models get higher weight.

docs.title: Live Integration
docs.text: Connect the optimizer to a live Slurm or PBS cluster for real-time prediction and feedback tracking.

docs.title: Slurm Integration
docs.text:
from pathlib import Path
from hpcopt.integrations.slurm_connector import SlurmConnector

connector = SlurmConnector(
    model_dir=Path("outputs/models/latest"),
    lookback_hours=24,  # Fetch last 24h of jobs
)

result = connector.sync()
print(f"Jobs ingested: {result.jobs_ingested}")
print(f"Predictions made: {result.recommendations_generated}")

docs.title: CLI Usage
docs.text:
python -m hpcopt.integrations.slurm_connector \
    --model-dir outputs/models/latest \
    --lookback-hours 48 \
    --output sync_report.json

docs.title: PBS Pro Integration
docs.text:
from hpcopt.integrations.pbs_connector import PBSConnector

connector = PBSConnector(
    model_dir=Path("outputs/models/latest"),
)
result = connector.sync()

docs.title: Prediction Feedback Loop
docs.text:
Track prediction accuracy and detect model drift:

from hpcopt.integrations.feedback import FeedbackTracker

tracker = FeedbackTracker(store_path=Path("outputs/feedback"))

# Record each completed job
tracker.record(
    job_id=12345,
    predicted={"p10": 60, "p50": 120, "p90": 300},
    actual_sec=145,
)

# Check model health
report = tracker.generate_report()
print(f"Coverage: {report.interval_coverage:.0%}")
print(f"Drift: {report.drift_severity}")
print(f"Action: {report.recommendation}")

Drift Detection Thresholds

  • Warning (mild): Coverage drops below 70%
  • Critical (severe): Coverage drops below 50%

When drift is detected, the system recommends model retraining.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: Get from zero to benchmark results in under 5 minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.11+
  • Rust 1.70+ (optional, for Rust sim-runner)

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/ErenAri/HCP-workload-optimizer.git
cd HCP-workload-optimizer

# Install with pip (editable mode)
pip install -e ".[dev]"

# Optional: LightGBM for 50× faster training
pip install -e ".[lightgbm]"

docs.title: Download Sample Data
docs.text:
The project uses traces from the Parallel Workloads Archive:

mkdir -p data/raw
# CTC-SP2 (77K jobs)
curl -o data/raw/CTC-SP2-1996-3.1-cln.swf.gz \
    https://www.cs.huji.ac.il/labs/parallel/workload/l_ctc_sp2/CTC-SP2-1996-3.1-cln.swf.gz

docs.title: Step 1: Ingest Trace
docs.text:
hpcopt ingest swf \
    --input data/raw/CTC-SP2-1996-3.1-cln.swf.gz \
    --dataset-id ctc_sp2 \
    --out outputs/curated \
    --report-out outputs/reports

This produces:

  • outputs/curated/ctc_sp2.parquet — canonical job records
  • outputs/curated/ctc_sp2_features.parquet — engineered features
  • outputs/reports/ctc_sp2_quality.json — ingestion quality report

docs.title: Step 2: Train Model
docs.text:
hpcopt train runtime \
    --input outputs/curated/ctc_sp2_features.parquet \
    --model-id ctc_sp2_model \
    --out outputs/models

Use LightGBM for 50× faster training

If LightGBM is installed, it's automatically used as the default backend. Training drops from ~6 minutes to ~8 seconds.

docs.title: Step 3: Run Simulation
docs.text:
PythonRust (16,000× faster) 
hpcopt simulate \
    --input outputs/curated/ctc_sp2.parquet \
    --policy EASY_BACKFILL_BASELINE \
    --capacity-cpus 512 \
    --out outputs/reports

cd rust && cargo build --release
./target/release/sim-runner \
    --input ../outputs/curated/ctc_sp2.json \
    --policy EASY_BACKFILL_BASELINE \
    --capacity-cpus 512 \
    --output ../outputs/reports/rust_report.json

docs.title: Step 4: View Results
docs.text: python scripts/benchmark_suite.py

docs.title: RL Policy Search
docs.text: The RL policy search module finds optimal scheduling parameters by running simulations with different configurations and selecting the best one based on p95 Bounded Slowdown.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The RL environment models scheduling as a decision problem:

  • State: Queue depth, utilization, wait times, completion progress
  • Action: Backfill threshold, short-job priority boost, starvation cap
  • Reward: Negative BSLD + utilization bonus

docs.title: Grid Search
docs.text:
The most practical approach for this 3-parameter action space:

from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
from hpcopt.simulate.rl_env import SchedulingEnv, grid_search_policy

# Load trace
df = pd.read_json("outputs/curated/ctc_sp2.json")

# Create environment
env = SchedulingEnv(trace_df=df, capacity_cpus=512)

# Search
best_action, best_result, all_results = grid_search_policy(
    env,
    bf_thresholds=[0.0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1.0],
    short_boosts=[0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0],
)

print(f"Best p95_BSLD: {best_result.p95_bsld:.2f}")
print(f"Backfill threshold: {best_action.backfill_threshold:.2f}")
print(f"Short-job boost: {best_action.priority_boost_short:.2f}")

docs.title: Random Search
docs.text:
For broader exploration with more configurations:

from hpcopt.simulate.rl_env import random_search_policy

best_action, best_result = random_search_policy(
    env, n_trials=200, seed=42
)

docs.title: Results on CTC-SP2
docs.text:
Policy p95 BSLD vs EASY_BACKFILL FIFO 173.13 — EASY_BACKFILL 3.82 baseline RL-Optimized 2.85 +25.5%

The optimal policy found: full backfill (threshold=1.0) with strong short-job priority boost (boost=1.0).

docs.title: Running the Script
docs.text:
python scripts/rl_policy_search.py

Results are saved to outputs/benchmark/rl_search/grid_search_results.json.

docs.title: Rust Simulation Engine
docs.text: The Rust sim-runner provides 16,000–51,000× speedup over the Python simulator for large-scale policy evaluation.

docs.title: Building
docs.text:
cd rust/sim-runner
cargo build --release

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
./target/release/sim-runner \
    --input trace.json \
    --policy EASY_BACKFILL_BASELINE \
    --capacity-cpus 512 \
    --output report.json

docs.title: Supported Policies
docs.text: Policy Description FIFO_STRICT First-in-first-out, no backfill EASY_BACKFILL_BASELINE EASY backfill with shadow time reservation

docs.title: Input Format
docs.text:
JSON array of job objects:

[
  {
    "job_id": 1,
    "submit_ts": 1000000,
    "runtime_actual_sec": 3600,
    "requested_cpus": 4
  }
]

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text:
{
  "policy_id": "EASY_BACKFILL_BASELINE",
  "metrics": {
    "total_jobs": 77222,
    "p95_bsld": 3.94,
    "utilization": 0.555,
    "mean_wait_sec": 1883,
    "p95_wait_sec": 13045
  }
}

docs.title: Python Bridge
docs.text:
from hpcopt.simulate.rust_bridge import run_rust_simulation

result = run_rust_simulation(
    trace_path="trace.json",
    policy="EASY_BACKFILL_BASELINE",
    capacity_cpus=512,
)
print(result["metrics"]["p95_bsld"])

The bridge auto-discovers the Rust binary and falls back gracefully.
